meta_title: Military Buyback Calculator for Federal Employees | Capital Wealth
meta_description: Calculate the exact cost of buying back your military service and the lifetime pension value it unlocks. Free tool for federal employees with military service. Built with Ann Werts.
hero_subhead: See exactly what your military buyback costs — and what that $4,200 returns to you in lifetime federal pension dollars. Built for federal employees with prior military service.
status_copy: We're finalizing the math with Ann Werts against the latest DFAS interest schedules. Drop your email below and we'll send you the calculator the day it launches — plus a 3-page explainer on the 3-year interest-free window most feds miss.

value_bullets.title: Calculate your exact buyback cost
value_bullets.body: Enter your military basic pay history and we'll compute the 3% deposit — plus any accrued DFAS interest if you're past your 3-year window.

value_bullets.title: Project the lifetime pension ROI
value_bullets.body: See what adding those years does to your FERS formula — at your high-3, at your service computation date, across a 25- or 30-year retirement horizon.

value_bullets.title: Find your break-even month
value_bullets.body: Most military buybacks pay for themselves in year one of retirement. Yours might be faster. The calculator shows the exact month you break even.

value_bullets.title: Flag the interest-free deadline
value_bullets.body: You have 2 years and 364 days of federal service to complete a buyback interest-free. After that, the math gets ugly fast. We'll tell you where you stand.

context_headline: Why military buyback is almost always a win
context_paragraphs: If you served in the military and you're not already drawing a military retirement, a buyback is usually one of the highest-ROI moves you can make as a federal employee. You pay 3% of your military basic pay (not your civilian salary — your actual military pay for those years). In exchange, those years count toward your FERS service computation date, your pension formula, and your eligibility thresholds for retirement.

We've seen buybacks cost $4,000 and unlock a full additional year of federal pension — every year, for life. On a $75,000 high-3, that's roughly $750 more in annual pension, which over a 25-year retirement works out to about $18,750 in today's dollars. For a $4,000 outlay.

The catch is timing. You have 2 years and 364 days of federal employment to complete the buyback interest-free. After that, DFAS starts charging variable interest — and the longer you wait, the more it compounds. The calculator flags whether you're inside or outside the window.

meta_title: FERS Sick Leave Conversion Calculator | Capital Wealth
meta_description: Your unused sick leave converts to creditable federal service at retirement. See exactly how many months it adds to your pension — and what that's worth in lifetime dollars. Free calculator.
hero_subhead: Your unused sick leave hours convert to months and days of creditable federal service when you retire. That's extra pension — for life. See what yours is worth.
status_copy: We're pairing this with OPM's official 2087-hour conversion chart and lifetime pension projections. Drop your email below — we'll send you the calculator the day it launches.

value_bullets.title: Convert your hours into creditable service
value_bullets.body: Enter your current sick-leave balance. We'll show you exactly how many months and days OPM will add to your service computation date using the official conversion chart.

value_bullets.title: Project the pension impact
value_bullets.body: See what the added months do to your FERS pension formula — at your high-3 and your service years. Most feds are surprised by how much it is.

value_bullets.title: Calculate the lifetime dollar value
value_bullets.body: Across a 25- or 30-year retirement, a healthy sick-leave balance can be worth $15,000 to $50,000+ in additional pension. See your specific number.

value_bullets.title: Compare burn vs. bank scenarios
value_bullets.body: The break-even math for taking sick leave now versus banking it for retirement. (Spoiler: banking usually wins by a lot, but the calculator tells you when it doesn't.)

context_headline: Why most feds leave thousands on the table here
context_paragraphs: Banking sick leave isn't just goodwill. At retirement, every hour converts into creditable federal service — meaning a bigger pension check, for life. The conversion uses OPM's official 2,087-hour chart: roughly 174 hours = one month of service credit. A balance of 1,500 hours adds about 8.5 months to your service computation date.

On a typical FERS pension of $50,000/year, 8 extra months of service credit is worth about $330 in annual pension. Doesn't sound like much — until you multiply by 25 years of retirement. That's $8,250 in today's dollars, from leave you weren't going to use anyway.

Balances of 2,500+ hours aren't unusual for career feds. At that level you're adding more than a year of service credit — often $20,000 to $40,000+ in lifetime pension value. Stop burning sick leave you don't need.

email_capture_sub: We'll email you the moment the tool goes live — plus a 2-page explainer on OPM's sick-leave conversion rules and the common edge cases (CSRS offset, part-time service) that trip people up.

meta_title: FERS 1.1% Multiplier Decision Tool | Capital Wealth
meta_description: Retiring at age 62 with 20+ years of service bumps your FERS pension multiplier from 1.0% to 1.1% — a 10% raise, for life. See if working a few more months is worth it. Free tool.
hero_subhead: Retiring at age 62 with 20+ years of service bumps your multiplier from 1.0% to 1.1% — a 10% raise on your pension, for life. See if working a few extra months is worth it.
status_copy: We're pairing the pension math with a realistic cost-of-working model (commute, FEHB while working, TSP contributions, lost Social Security) so you see the net picture, not just the pension side. Drop your email — we'll send you the tool the day it launches.

value_bullets.title: Show your pension with and without the 1.1%
value_bullets.body: At your current SCD and high-3, see the exact pension figure at 1.0% versus 1.1%. Monthly, annually, and across 25 and 30 years of retirement.

value_bullets.title: Calculate the break-even date
value_bullets.body: How many extra months do you need to work to earn back the pension you gave up by not retiring today? For most feds it's 14–22 months. Yours is specific.

value_bullets.title: Factor in lost retirement income
value_bullets.body: Working an extra year means a year of pension you didn't receive, a year without Social Security, and a year of continued FEHB premiums out of pocket. The tool nets it all out.

value_bullets.title: Flag the edge cases
value_bullets.body: Special Retirement Supplement eligibility, MRA+30 interactions, and the cliff at 62 where the multiplier changes. We'll tell you if you're in one of them.

context_headline: Why one extra year at the office can fund a decade of retirement
context_paragraphs: Your FERS pension formula is simple on paper: years of service × multiplier × high-3 salary. The multiplier is 1.0% unless you retire at age 62 or later with 20 or more years of service — then it jumps to 1.1%. That's a 10% raise on your pension, for the rest of your life.

For a career fed with a $90,000 high-3 and 25 years of service, the difference is significant. 1.0% × 25 × $90,000 = $22,500/year. 1.1% × 25 × $90,000 = $24,750/year. Across a 25-year retirement, that extra 10% is $56,250 in today's dollars — just from picking a different retirement date.

The tradeoff: you have to keep working. For most feds, the math works out to 14–22 months of continued employment to break even, but your specific break-even depends on your commute costs, your TSP matching, and whether you'd be drawing Social Security in the alternative. The tool runs the whole thing.
email_capture_headline: Send me the tool when it launches
email_capture_sub: We'll email you the moment the tool goes live — plus a short PDF explainer on the 1.1% multiplier rules and the 3 edge cases (MRA+30, postponed annuity, disability retirement) where it doesn't apply.
